subject: Physics
solution:
Initial balance at 40 cm: $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{40}{60} = \frac{2}{3}$ ...(i)
With $16\,\Omega$ parallel to $R_2$, null point at 50 cm:
$\frac{R_1}{\frac{16R_2}{16+R_2}} = \frac{50}{50} = 1 \Rightarrow R_1 = \frac{16R_2}{16+R_2}$ ...(ii)
From (i): $R_1 = \frac{2}{3}R_2$.
Substituting in (ii): $\frac{2}{3}R_2 = \frac{16R_2}{16+R_2}$.
$\frac{2}{3} = \frac{16}{16+R_2} \Rightarrow 2(16+R_2) = 48 \Rightarrow R_2 = 8\,\Omega$.
$R_1 = \frac{16}{3}\,\Omega$.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let r be the internal resistance of each cell.

In above shown figure, first represents the series connection of cells. Second and third figures represents the parallel connection of cells.

Now, in case of series connection, εnet=2ε and rnet=2r. So, current i=2ε5+2r .....(1)

In case of parallel connection, εnet=εr+εrr+r=ε and rnet=r2. So, current i=εr2+5 .....(2)

Equating (1) and (2), we get

2ε5+2r=εr2+5

⇒r+10=5+2r

⇒r=5 Ω.

subject: Physics
options: A is not correct but R is correct
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is not correct
solution:
Voltmeter is connected in parallel, so it should have very high resistance as to measure the potential difference, all the current should pass through the resistor and no current should pass through voltmeter for precise readings.
The current will divide and travel through the voltmeter if the voltmeter has a low resistance and will then give faulty readings.

Thus, A is not correct but R is correct.

subject: Physics
solution:
Given, the resistance of the galvanometer G=100 Ω
No. of divisions =50
Current Sensitivity  =20 μA/division

The maximum current through galvanometer will be 
Imax=50×20×10-6=10-3 A
For V1 = 2 V

using Ohm's law V1= Imax G+R1
2=10-3 ×100+R1
∴R1=1900 Ω

For V2 = 10 V

using Ohm's law V2 =ImaxG+R1+R2

10=10-3 ×100 + 1900+R2
∴R2=8000 Ω

For V3 =20 V

using Ohm's law V3 =Imax G+R1+R2+R3
20=10-3 100 +1900+8000+R3
∴R3=10×103 Ω

subject: Physics
solution:
The resistance of a wire (R) is related to its resistivity ρ, length(l)  and cross-sectional area A as:

R=ρlA

As the length of wire increases, the cross-sectional area decreases. The cross-sectional area in terms of volume (V) and length (l) is

A=Vl

∴ R=ρl2V

Taking the volume of wire constant,

R∝l2

So if R' is the resistance of wire after getting redrawn and R is the initial resistance of wire,

∴ R'R=(5l)2l2=25⇒R'=25×R=125 Ω

subject: Physics
solution:
Given initially a resistance P = 4 Ω is connected in the left gap and let Q is connected in the right gap of meter bridge and the null point is obtained at 60 cm,

From the balanced condition of the Wheatstone bridge 

PQ=RS  ...1

Let ρ is the resistance per unit length of meter bridge wire than

For wire AN, P=ρl=40 ρ   ∵ l = 40 cm 

And for NB wire 

Q = ρ100-lQ= ρ100-60=40 ρ  

On substituting the values from equation 1

460=Q40

⇒Q=166=83 Ω

Now an unknown resistance R is connected in series with the 4 Ω resistance and the null point is obtained at l=80 cm 

Now, P = ρ l =80 l

and Q = ρ100-lQ= ρ100-80= 20 ρ

Then from equation 1

 4+R80=Q20⇒ 4+R80=8320  ∵ Q=83⇒4+R80=860  ⇒ R = 203 Ω

subject: Physics
solution:
The resistance of the bulb is calculated using its power rating:

$R_b = \dfrac{V^2}{P} = \dfrac{200^2}{100} = 400 \, \Omega$

The bulb is connected in parallel with the $400 \, \Omega$ resistor. The equivalent resistance of this parallel combination is:

$R_p = \dfrac{400 \times 400}{400 + 400} = 200 \, \Omega$

This parallel combination is in series with the $200 \, \Omega$ resistor. The total equivalent resistance of the circuit is:

$R_{eq} = 200 + R_p = 200 + 200 = 400 \, \Omega$

The current drawn from the battery is:

$I = \dfrac{V}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{100}{400} = 0.25 \text{ A}$

The potential drop across the bulb is the same as the potential drop across the parallel combination:

$V_p = I \times R_p = 0.25 \times 200 = 50 \text{ V}$

Answer: $50$

subject: Physics
solution:
Meter bridge null point condition: $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{l}{100-l}$
Initial: $R_1 = 2Ω$, $R_2 = 3Ω$, null at $l$ cm
$\frac{2}{3} = \frac{l}{100-l}$ gives $2(100-l) = 3l$, so $l = 40$ cm
When $xΩ$ connected in parallel with $3Ω$: $R_2' = \frac{3x}{3+x}Ω$
New null point at $(40+10) = 50$ cm from left:
$\frac{2}{R_2'} = \frac{50}{50} = 1$
$\frac{2(3+x)}{3x} = 1$ gives $6 + 2x = 3x$
$x = 6Ω$

subject: Physics
solution: From symmetry, current through e-b & g-d =0 $\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{R}_{\text {eq }}=\frac{3}{4} \times \mathrm{R}=\frac{3}{2} \Omega \\ & \therefore \text { Current through battery }=\frac{6 \times 2}{3}=4 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{i}_2=\frac{4}{8} \times 2=1 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \therefore \Delta \mathrm{V} \text { across e-f }=\frac{\mathrm{i}_2}{2} \times \mathrm{R}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2=1 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given $V = 10\text{ V}$ and $I = 5\text{ A}$

Least count of voltmeter, $\Delta V = 500\text{ mV} = 0.5\text{ V}$

Least count of ammeter, $\Delta I = 200\text{ mA} = 0.2\text{ A}$

Resistance is given by Ohm's law:

$R = \dfrac{V}{I} = \dfrac{10}{5} = 2\text{ }\Omega$

The maximum permissible relative error in resistance is:

$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V} + \dfrac{\Delta I}{I}$

$\dfrac{\Delta R}{2} = \dfrac{0.5}{10} + \dfrac{0.2}{5}$

$\dfrac{\Delta R}{2} = 0.05 + 0.04 = 0.09$

$\Delta R = 2 \times 0.09 = 0.18\text{ }\Omega$

Answer: $0.18$

solution:
As can be seen from the first circuit above, CB & AB are in series which is in parallel with CA. So, the net resistance is

R'=62 Ω=3  Ω as shown in the second diagram.

From the second diagram, DC & CA is in series which is parallel to DA. Hence the resistance is

R''=6×3(6+3) Ω=2 Ω as shown in third diagram.

From third diagram, both ED & DA are in series which is parallel to EA.

The equivalent resistance as can be seen from the above simplified circuit is Req=2 Ω.

 The current I through battery, I= 82=4 A.

From the second diagram, the part EDACD has a resistance of 4 Ω, the total current will divide equally between EDACD & EA. So consider I1=2 A flowing through ED. Note that I1 will be the same current flowing in the first diagram through ED.

From first diagram, when the resistances are in parallel the current is divided in the inverse ratio. Thus,
I' through CD=39×2=23 A
I'' through R2=1223=13 A
